## Page 357

### [p357_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p357_c0002] Long Island Rail Road Transit Monitoring
[p357_c0003] The FTA requires that transit providers, such as the MTA and its affiliated agencies, monitor, not less than every three (3) years, the performance of their transit system relative to their system-wide service standards and service policies. The Long Island Rail Road annual monitoring report is prepared and submitted to the MTA Department of Diversity and Civil Rights as part of its monitoring and oversight of MTA's operating agencies Title VI compliance. The Data contained herein becomes part of the MTA-wide consolidated Title VI Program reviewed and approved by all internal stakeholders, including the MTA Board of Directors and submitted to the Federal Transit Administration (FTA) on a triennial basis. (Exhibit 1).
[p357_c0004] MTA's Board approved standards and policies are presented in detail in a previous section of this Report entitled "Requirement to Set System-wide Service Standards and Policies" and include standards and policies for such factors as: Vehicle Load, Vehicle Headway, On-Time Performance, Service Availability, Transit Amenities and Vehicle Assignment.
[p357_c0005] Each of these six (6) factors, are monitored statistically, and separately for all modes, using a combination of quantitative techniques: the statistical t-test, the chi-square statistical test and ridership surveys which help to define the actual experiences of individual passengers. The MTA uses these quantitative measures of testing to ascertain if there are significant differences between either minority/non-minority and/or income groups being measured for service equity analyses. Use of either of the two statistical tests is dependent on the type of data being analyzed.
### [p357_c0006] Assessments:
[p357_c0007] As a summary of the Long Island Rail Road July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025, Title VI associated quantitative analyses of the current service procedures (standards) the following assessment of compliance, predicated on the minority based comparisons is presented. For the sake of brevity, we have presented the findings in the tables below.
[p357_c0008] Detailed analytical data, showing the numerical calculations is available upon request. Please note, in the case of an identified inequity, then Long Island Rail Road will show that inequity with either its proposed corrective action or the reason why corrective action is not appropriate, that is, the inequity is neither a
[p357_c0009] _Page 2_
[p357_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p357_c0011] _Master Page # 357 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

[p358_c0002] direct result of any Long Island Rail Road related service action nor a violation of approved MTA standards and policies.

## Page 359

### [p359_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p359_c0002] Long Island Rail Road Service Standards and Policies Monitoring Summary
[p359_c0003]

```text
Service Standards and Policies Service Measure Standard Analysis Results – Minority Analysis Results – Poverty Analysis Notes/Mitigation Service Availability The LIRR Service Area consists of all Census tracts within (and touching) a 2.5-mile radius from all Stations. Per thresholds developed for the NYC and Long Island service areas, minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty designations are assigned to each tract. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between the station distance for randomly selected minority/non-minority tracts and above/at-or-below poverty tracts. t-test No significant difference No significant difference Vehicle Load* LIRR Service Standards include guideline load factors of .95 for the AM Peak and .85 for Off-Peak A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between load factors on minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference No significant difference Vehicle Headway* LIRR Service Standards include maximum headway guidelines by station level of service and time of day. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between average headways on minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference Statistically significant difference during off-peak (Favorable to Low Income) Lower headways on low-income branches at all times. On-time Performance* Measure of trains arriving at their final destination within 5:59 of their scheduled arrival time. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between the On-time Performance for minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference No significant difference Vehicle Assignment* LIRR assigns its passenger fleet to branches dependent on the propulsion type on that branch. Both electric and diesel cars are assigned as required without regard to car age. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between the fleet age for cars that serve minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference No significant difference
```
[p359_c0004] *Vehicle Load, Vehicle Headway, On-time Performance, and Vehicle Assignment are all service measures that are monitored at the branch level. Pursuant to the FTA Title VI circular, a branch is designated as a Minority Branch if at least one-third of its total route mileage falls in one or more minority Census tracts. A branch is designated as an At-or-Below Poverty Branch if at least one-third of its total route mileage falls into one or more at-or-below poverty Census tracts.
[p359_c0005] _Page 4_

## Page 360

### [p360_c0001] Long Island Rail Road Title VI Program Update July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025
### [p360_c0002] Exhibit 11: Performance Monitoring Samples
### [p360_c0003] Part I: Introduction, Test Method, and Summary of Results
[p360_c0004] _Master Page # 360 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 361

### [p361_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p361_c0002] Introduction
[p361_c0003] The Long Island Rail Road monitors the performance of our transit system relative to the Board-approved system-wide service standards and service policies and reports annually on the results of its monitoring efforts to the MTA's Department of Diversity and Civil Rights (DDCR). A triennial report is also prepared for inclusion in the MTA-wide submission to the FTA.
[p361_c0004] The Long Island Rail Road uses quantitative measures of testing: the t-test and Chi-square statistical tests, in order to ascertain if there are significant differences between either minority /non-minority and/or income groups being measured for service equity analyses. Use of either of these two statistical tests is dependent on the type of data being analyzed. The service equity monitoring showed that the application of the Standards and Policies resulted in no disparate impact or disproportionate burden on minority or low-income communities.
[p361_c0005] Long Island Rail Road monitoring assessments, which are used to determine whether disparate impacts exist on the basis of race, color, or national origin, were completed for each of the board- approved standards and policies, for the minority and non-minority populations. These assessments are detailed, along with the monitoring results, in Parts II and III of this Exhibit.
### [p361_c0006] Test Method
[p361_c0007] LIRR uses the following techniques to monitor the performance of the transit system relative to system-wide service standards and policies:
- [p361_c0008] * Service Availability: A t-test statistical analysis is used to see if there was any difference in the distances to the closest railroad stations, between the randomly selected minority & non-minority populations and for the randomly selected above poverty and at-or-below poverty populations. * Vehicle Load: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the services provided for the minority branches and the non-minority branches and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty branches. * Vehicle Headway: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the level of service provided for the minority routes and the non-minority routes and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty routes. * On-Time Performance: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the on-time performance of vehicles on the minority routes and the non-minority routes and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty routes. * Vehicle Assignment: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the average age of the equipment used on the minority branch and the non-minority branches and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty routes. * Transit Amenities: Chi-Square analysis is performed on station amenities with established standards and guidelines to determine if there is any difference in amenity distribution between the stations used in the minority and non-minority Census tracts and those used in the above poverty and at-or-below poverty tracts.
[p361_c0009] _Page 6_
[p361_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p361_c0011] _Master Page # 361 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 367

### [p367_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p367_c0002] A.1. Minority-Based Analysis – Service Availability
### [p367_c0003] Data
[p367_c0004] Service Availability is a general measure of the distribution of routes within the MTA Long Island Rail Road (LIRR) service area.
[p367_c0005] In order to compare the distance traveled to gain access to LIRR service between the minority and non-minority areas, the New York City and Long Island census tracts were separated into minority and non-minority tracts.
[p367_c0006] LIRR service area includes all census tracts that are within (and touching) 2.5 miles of all LIRR railroad stations in Manhattan, Queens, Brooklyn, Nassau and Suffolk counties.
[p367_c0007] For the LIRR New York City service area (of the 5 counties making up New York City, the LIRR serves 3: Queens, Kings (Brooklyn) and New York (Manhattan)), a census tract is considered minority if the minority population of the tracts selected in the service area is equal to or greater than the service area's minority population of 67.66%. For the Long Island counties of Nassau and Suffolk service area (the LIRR serves both), a census tract is considered minority if the minority population of that tract is equal to, or greater than the Long Island service area's minority population of 39.65%. Statistics were taken from the Census Bureau's 5 year American Communities Survey (2018-2022).
[p367_c0008] In order to compare the distance traveled to gain access to LIRR service between minority and non-minority areas, the New York City and Long Island census tracts were separated into minority and non-minority tracts.
[p367_c0009] 200 of all the LIRR service area census tracts were randomly selected for analysis (100 from the minority tracts and 100 from the non-minority tracts). The distance from the centroid of each census tract to the closest LIRR station was measured. Additionally, our sampling methodology was done by region, (20% NYC and 80% LI service areas) reflecting LIRR ridership patterns.
[p367_c0010] Table 1 lists each randomly selected tract number (grouped by minority status), the distance to the closest LIRR station from the centroid of the tract, and the closest LIRR station
[p367_c0011] _Page 12_
[p367_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p367_c0013] _Master Page # 367 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 378

### [p378_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p378_c0002] A.1. Minority-Based Analysis – Service Availability (Continued)
### [p378_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p378_c0004] To determine if the LIRR service provided is equitable with respect to minority and non-minority census tracts, a comparison of service availability was performed. A t -test statistical analysis was used to see if there was any difference in the distances to the closest railroad stations, between the randomly selected minority & non-minority populations.
[p378_c0005] Based on the results below, we can conclude that there appears to be no inequity between the minority and the non-minority populations as it relates to service availability in the LIRR service areas.
[p378_c0006]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Mean Distance (miles) 1.31 1.46 Standard Deviation (miles) 0.73 0.75
```
[p378_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (for a 95% confidence), the resulting t -statistic = -1.38 . The t -critical values are +/- 1.97. Since -1.38 > -1.97 and < +1.97, we can therefore conclude that there is no statistical difference between the distance minority populations and non-minority populations in the LIRR service area are to LIRR stations.
[p378_c0008] _Page 23_
[p378_c0009] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p378_c0010] _Master Page # 378 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 379

### [p379_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p379_c0002] A.2. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Load Factor
### [p379_c0003] Data
[p379_c0004] The Long Island Rail Road load factor standards are designed to be responsive to passenger demands. The overall load factor standard is one (1) train car for every 115* boarding passengers on M3 electric cars, one (1) train car for every 106* boarding passengers on M7 and M9 electric cars. As of June 30, 2025, 9% of the available MU (multiple unit) fleet was M3 cars, 73% was M7 cars and 18% was M9 cars. Therefore, the average number of seats per car was calculated as 107.5.
[p379_c0005] In addition, the overall load factor standard is one (1) train car for every 140 boarding passengers on diesel bi-level cars per station east of Jamaica. A review of train size is initiated when train loading exceeds or falls below established load point factors. The LIRR goal is to provide adequate seating during the morning and evening peak period. The Long Island Rail Road monitors and reviews vehicle load data on an on-going basis and, wherever feasible, reallocates human and equipment resources to meet passenger demand.
[p379_c0006] Table 1 is the load factor analysis based on the 2024 Ridership Count 1 . This table illustrates that the LIRR has been responsive to passenger seating demands. The table shows the peak one (1) hour period load factor for all branches on the LIRR. Peak hour trains are defined as those trains arriving at the western terminals, or providing connecting service to trains arriving at western terminals, between 8:00 a.m. and 9:00 a.m.
[p379_c0007] Table 2 shows the off-peak period load factor for all branches on the LIRR. Off-Peak is defined as those trains arriving at the western terminals, or providing connecting service to trains arriving at western terminals, between 10:00 a.m. and 4:00 p.m.
[p379_c0008] Note: A branch is denoted as a Minority Branch if at least one third of its total route mileage falls in one or more minority census tracts. Any station that is located within a minority census tract is considered a minority station.
[p379_c0009] *Number of seats on M3, M7 and M9 cars is an average. M7 and M9 cars seating varies, one car in each pair is equipped with a handicapped accessible restroom and therefore has less seats than its mate. In addition, minor modifications have been undertaken to increase seating on some M7 cars.
[p379_c0010] _1 Data that was available as of 6/30/2024_
[p379_c0011] _Page 24_
[p379_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p379_c0013] _Master Page # 379 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 382

### [p382_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p382_c0002] A.2. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Load Factor (Continued)
### [p382_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p382_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the services provided for the minority branches and the non-minority branches. The results for the am peak and off peak time periods are listed below.
### [p382_c0005] AM PEAK
[p382_c0006]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Guideline Average Load Factor 0.95 0.95 Actual Average Load Factor 0.59 0.69 Average Difference 0.36 0.26 Variance 0.023 0.004
```
[p382_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 1.50 . The t-critical values are +/-2.36. Since 1.50 > -2.36 and < +2.36, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the AM peak train service provided between the minority and non-minority branches.
### [p382_c0008] OFF PEAK
[p382_c0009]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Guideline Average Load Factor 0.85 0.85 Actual Average Load Factor 0.23 0.22 Average Difference 0.62 0.63 Variance 0.00 0.00
```
[p382_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -0.14 . The t-critical values are +/-2.26. Since -0.14 > -2.26 and < +2.26, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the off peak train service provided between the minority and non-minority branches.
[p382_c0011] _Page 27_
[p382_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p382_c0013] _Master Page # 382 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 383

### [p383_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p383_c0002] A.3. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Headway
### [p383_c0003] Data
[p383_c0004] In determining train headways, many factors are considered. The first is ridership demand. Service is scheduled to ensure that, where possible, all customers can expect a seated ride to their destination. Minimum levels of service to ensure customer convenience and comfort are based on Long Island Rail Road Service Guidelines. Annual ridership checks are performed on all scheduled trains to determine if LIRR service meets current ridership demand and Service Guideline standards.
[p383_c0005] In addition to ridership demand, vehicle headways are significantly influenced by crew and equipment availability. After determining the proper number of trains per hour based on ridership demand, the equipment to be operated and the crews to operate the equipment must be manipulated throughout the LIRR system to assure that all train intervals can be covered with maximum efficiency.
[p383_c0006] The number of available train slots within our system influences the number of trains that can be scheduled. Vehicle headways and capacity can be influenced by construction projects that affect infrastructure availability. The available infrastructure in a given area determines the maximum number of trains that can operate over a given portion of railroad. In some instances available time slots can also be impacted by service in the opposing direction. Vehicles headways are scheduled to meet Service Guidelines while assuring that the number and frequency of trains does not exceed the track and/or signal capacity in that given area.
[p383_c0007] The passenger train schedules provide the most current information as it pertains to the LIRR's vehicle headways. The schedules indicate the scheduled frequency of service provided by the LIRR. The LIRR considers the morning peak to be from 6:00 am until 10:00 am and the evening peak to be from 4:00 pm to 8:00 pm during the weekday.
[p383_c0008] The following table lists the actual average branch headways as of June 30, 2025.
[p383_c0009] _Page 28_
[p383_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p383_c0011] _Master Page # 383 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 384

### [p384_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p384_c0002] VEHICLE HEADWAY (minutes)
[p384_c0003]

```text
BRANCH AM PEAK PM PEAK OFF PEAK BABYLON 9 11 19 * FAR ROCKAWAY 17 28 53 * HEMPSTEAD 25 25 58 * HUNTINGTON 15 18 30 * LONG BEACH 18 24 55 OYSTER BAY 39 50 75 PATCHOGUE/SPEONK/MONTAUK 26 22 57 PORT JEFFERSON 27 33 71 * PORT WASHINGTON 16 13 29 RONKONKOMA ^^ 12 14 29 * WEST HEMPSTEAD 52 40 59
```

[p384_c0006] Notes: Train stopping patterns vary to equalize passenger loadings and minimize standees.
[p384_c0007] There are no headway minutes for Greenport during the AM and PM Peak because there is no continuous train service during the peak periods.
[p384_c0008] _Page 29_
[p384_c0009] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p384_c0010] _Master Page # 384 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 385

### [p385_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p385_c0002] A.3. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Headway
### [p385_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p385_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the level of service provided for the minority routes and the non-minority routes. We can conclude from the following analysis that there appears to be no inequity as it relates to average vehicle headway in LIRR’s service area. The results for the am peak and off peak time periods are listed below.
### [p385_c0005] AM PEAK
[p385_c0006]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Actual Average Headway (minutes) 23.83 22.60 Standard Deviation 13.01 10.93
```
[p385_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 0.15 . The t-critical values are +/-2.26. Since 0.15 > -2.26 and < +2.26, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the average headway during the peak period on the minority and non-minority branches.
### [p385_c0008] OFF PEAK
[p385_c0009]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Actual Average Headway (minutes) 47.33 50.20 Standard Deviation 12.76 22.44
```
[p385_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -0.23 . The t-critical values are +/-2.45. Since -0.23 > -2.45 and < +2.45, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the average headway during the off peak on the minority and non-minority branches.
[p385_c0011] _Page 30_
[p385_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p385_c0013] _Master Page # 385 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 386

### [p386_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p386_c0002] A.4. Minority-Based Analysis – On-Time Performance
### [p386_c0003] Data
[p386_c0004] On-time performance is a measure of runs completed as scheduled. At the Long Island Rail Road, a train is recorded as on time if it arrives at its destination within five minutes and 59 seconds of its scheduled arrival. 100% of trains operated should complete their assigned trips. Unless noted on the timetable, trains will not depart early from passenger stations where they are scheduled to receive passengers.
[p386_c0005] The table below lists the Peak and Off Peak Total Branch On-Time Performance for the year preceding June 30, 2025. This will be the basis of our equity analysis.
### [p386_c0006] TOTAL BRANCH ON-TIME PERFORMANCE
[p386_c0007] (7/1/22 - 06/30/25)
[p386_c0008]

```text
BRANCH AM PEAK OFF PEAK BABYLON 93.5 94.8 * FAR ROCKAWAY 95.4 98 * HEMPSTEAD 92.9 96.3 * HUNT. HICKS. 93.4 95.8 * LONG BEACH 94.9 97.6 MONTAUK 95 92.2 OYSTER BAY 94.4 94.6 PORT JEFFERSON 90.2 92.1 * PORT WASHINGTON 96.9 97.9 RONK. (GREENPT.) 93.1 97.9 * WEST HEMPSTEAD 94.7 96.5
```
[p386_c0009] * Designated as a minority branch
[p386_c0010] _Page 31_
[p386_c0011] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p386_c0012] _Master Page # 386 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 387

### [p387_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p387_c0002] A.4. Minority-Based Analysis – On-Time Performance
### [p387_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p387_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the on-time performance of vehicles on the minority routes and the non-minority routes. We can conclude from the following analysis that there appears to be no inequity as it relates to on-time performance in LIRR’s service area. The result of the analysis is listed below.
### [p387_c0005] Total On-Time Performance – AM PEAK
[p387_c0006]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Average On-Time Performance 94.7 93.4 Standard Deviation 0.01 .02
```
[p387_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 1.38 . The t-critical values are +/-2.23. Since 1.38 > -2.23 and <2.23, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the am peak average on-time performance of vehicles on the minority and non-minority branches.
### [p387_c0008] Total On-Time Performance – OFF PEAK
[p387_c0009]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Average On-Time Performance 96.75 95.15 Standard Deviation 0.02 0.02
```
[p387_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 2.00 . The t-critical values are +/-2.26. Since 2.00 > -2.26 and < +2.26, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the off peak average on-time performance of vehicles on the minority and non-minority branches.
[p387_c0011] _Page 32_
[p387_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p387_c0013] _Master Page # 387 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 388

### [p388_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p388_c0002] A.5. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Assignment
### [p388_c0003] Data
[p388_c0004] Electric Multiple Unit (MU) passenger cars are operated on the branches with electrification. As of June 30, 2025, the LIRR owned 1130 electric MU cars. 956 of these cars were required to operate morning rush hour service.
[p388_c0005] The portion of the line that is not electrified operates with diesel hauled locomotive coaches. As of June 30, 2025, the LIRR owned 134 diesel-hauled cars. A weekday average of 116 cars were required to operate the scheduled diesel service.
[p388_c0006] Both electric and diesel cars are assigned as required without regard to car age. Electric and diesel cars have the same amenities - air conditioning, heating and restrooms for passenger comfort and convenience. The average age of the M-3 electric cars is 38 years, the M-7 electric cars is 20 years, and the M-9 electric cars is 4.2 years. The average age of the diesel cars is 27 years.
[p388_c0007] Cars are not assigned to specific routes or branches within electric or diesel territory but are cycled from branch to branch and rotated based on a car maintenance schedule. To achieve optimum car utilization efficiency of both electric and diesel equipment, cars are cycled from branch to branch on a daily basis. For example, an early morning peak train from Babylon to Penn Station is cycled back to Port Washington for a second trip from Port Washington to Penn Station.
[p388_c0008] Table 1 is the Vehicle Assignment Record as of June 30, 2025.
[p388_c0009] _Page 33_
[p388_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p388_c0011] _Master Page # 388 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_


## Page 390

### [p390_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p390_c0002] A.5. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Assignment
### [p390_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p390_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the average age of the equipment used on the minority branch and the non-minority branches. The results for the am peak and off peak time periods are listed below. The results for both time periods are the same because the same equipment is used throughout the day.
### [p390_c0005] AM Peak
[p390_c0006]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Average Age (years) 18.6 23.66 Standard Deviation 0.00 4.10
```
[p390_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -2.45 . The t-critical values are +/-2.78. Since -2.45 > -2.78 and < +2.78, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the average age of the equipment used during the AM on the minority and non-minority branches.
### [p390_c0008] Off Peak
[p390_c0009]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Average Age (years) 18.6 23.66 Standard Deviation 0.00 4.10
```
[p390_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -2.45 . The t-critical values are +/-2.78. Since -2.45 > -2.78 and < +2.78, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the average age of the equipment used during the off-peak on the minority and non-minority branches.
[p390_c0011] _Page 35_
[p390_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p390_c0013] _Master Page # 390 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 391

### [p391_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p391_c0002] A.6. Minority-Based Analysis – Station Amenities
### [p391_c0003] Data
[p391_c0004] The following guidelines are primarily based on passenger utilization of stations and represent basic levels of service the LIRR will add to if necessary, commensurate with individual station needs:
### [p391_c0005] A. Station Levels of Service
[p391_c0006] The Station Levels of Service are a measure of the number of customers that utilize a particular station each weekday. The Long Island Rail Road uses Station Levels of Service to allocate the appropriate level of service for such areas as station maintenance, station amenities, schedule frequency, and other areas. Station Levels of Service provide an equitable way of allocating operating and capital resources within the LIRR. Station levels of service are based upon the data obtained from the most recent station-by-station boarding counts.
[p391_c0007] Level 1: More than 6,000 customers a day
[p391_c0008] Level 2: 2,000 – 6,000 customers a day
[p391_c0009] Level 3: 1,000 –1,999 customers a day
[p391_c0010] Level 4: 100 –999 customers a day
[p391_c0011] Level 5: Fewer than 100 customers a day
### [p391_c0012] B. Transit Access and Station Location
[p391_c0013] Transit access is a measure of the distance a person must travel to gain access to MTA Long Island Rail Road commuting services.
[p391_c0014] The decision to add, relocate, or close a passenger station on a permanent basis is a rare occurrence and is undertaken only after detailed evaluation of environmental, economic and operational impacts, and analysis of current station based boarding counts. These guidelines will serve to address shifting ridership patterns or opportunities associated with major service changes (e.g. electrification of a branch or branch segment).
[p391_c0015] When conditions exist that warrant development of new station sites, a review will be undertaken using the following criteria:
- [p391_c0016] • Ridership Potential • Operational Feasibility • Availability of Capital and Operating Funds • Community Impact • Environmental Justice impacts • Parking Availability • Proximity to existing LIRR stations • Other nearby transit options
[p391_c0017] When customer volume through a station falls below 400 passengers a day, a review will be initiated to determine the feasibility of diverting those passengers to neighboring stations and closing the station to thereby reduce trip time on the affected branch.
[p391_c0018] _Page 36_
[p391_c0019] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p391_c0020] _Master Page # 391 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 392

### [p392_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p392_c0002] C. Distribution of Transit Amenities
[p392_c0003] Transit amenities are items of comfort and convenience made available to the riding public at LIRR stations, including escalators, elevators, pedestrian overpasses / tunnels, waiting rooms, canopies/shelters, restrooms, staffed-ticket offices, ticket vending machines, platform shelters, public telephones, and vending concessions. The LIRR has developed station standards (shown below), which are utilized to determine the level of service that will be provided at each station. The level of amenities is based on station use level, i.e. the greater the number of passengers using a station on a daily basis, the greater the level of amenities provided at that station. Stations are categorized by level, ranging from Level 1 to Level 5, with Level 1 station serving more than 6,000 customers per day and a Level 4 station serving less than 100 customers per day. A Level 1 station receives a full range of transit amenities.
### [p392_c0004] D. Station Parking
[p392_c0005] Station parking projects are designed to improve vehicular access, to provide safer, more convenient intermodal transfers, to maximize parking capacity, and generally, to enhance the appearance of stations and surrounding areas.
[p392_c0006] Note: Local municipalities provide the majority of parking, some maintenance functions and various other services at LIRR stations. To support the municipal parking process, the LIRR provides station profiles to the responsible municipality when requested. Station profiles include characteristics of LIRR services offered at the station such as parking presently provided, parking lot lighting standards and present and projected station ridership data.
### [p392_c0007] E. Station Facilities
[p392_c0008] The following guidelines are primarily based on passenger utilization of stations and represent basic levels of service. The LIRR will add to if necessary, commensurate with individual station needs:
[p392_c0009] Level 1: More than 6,000 customers a day
[p392_c0010] Level 2: 2,000 – 6,000 customers a day
[p392_c0011] Level 3: 1,000 –1,999 customers a day
[p392_c0012] Level 4: 100 –999 customers a day
[p392_c0013] Level 5: Fewer than 100 customers a day
[p392_c0014] _Page 37_
[p392_c0015] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p392_c0016] _Master Page # 392 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 393

### [p393_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
[p393_c0002] The following matrix defines station facilities provided for each of the 5 Station Levels of Service:
[p393_c0003]

```text
Level 1 Level 2 Level 3 Level 4/5 Staffed Ticket Office X Public Restrooms X X Station Waiting Room X X X Ticket Vending Machine X X X X* Public Address Sytem X X X X Platform Shelters X X X X Customer Signage / Transit Information Center X X X X Platform Lighting X X X X
```
[p393_c0004] Notes:
- [p393_c0005] * Stations without TVMs include Mets-Willets Pt, Belmont Park, Pinelawn, Bellport, Yaphank, Riverhead, Mattituck, Southold, and Greenport. 1. There are three types of TVMs – Full Service TVMs which sell one-way, round-trip, weekly, ten-trip, and monthly tickets, as well as pre-valued MetroCards, and UniTicket. Daily TVMs sell one-way and round trip tickets and pre-valued MetroCards only. Cashless TVMs which sell all the ticket types that the Full Service TVMs offer, but accept only credit, debit and approved pre-tax commuter benefit cards. 2. Station waiting rooms are heated facilities; platform shelters are not. Some station platform waiting rooms are heated. 3. It is not the intent of the LIRR to reduce existing passenger amenities. Therefore, services will continue to be provided when station waiting rooms exist at level 4 stations and where public lavatories exist at level 3 and level 4 stations. 4. Special Event Stations are not included in the station level rankings as they do not have year-round train service. As a minimum, these stations will have passenger shelters, platforms, lighting in the vicinity of the shelters, and station signs. Other services will be provided as station use warrants.
### [p393_c0006] F. Station Accessibility
[p393_c0007] The LIRR is committed to complying with Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA) legislation and strives to make its rail system accessible to individuals with disabilities.
[p393_c0008] Of the 126 stations in the LIRR system, 116 of these stations have elevators and/or ramps to provide access to customers who use wheelchairs or other mobility aids. For example all stations on the Long Beach, West Hempstead (except St Albans), Montauk, Oyster Bay, and Ronkonkoma branches are wheelchair-accessible. Stations that are wheelchair-accessible are designated as such on all LIRR maps and timetables.
[p393_c0009] In addition to providing elevators and/or ramps, many stations have some of the following accessibility features, and all features are present at many stations:
- [p393_c0010] • ADA compliant handrails on ramps, stairs, and platforms • Braille signage • Audio and visual information systems • Accessible station ticket windows • Accessible Ticket Vending Machines (TVMs) • Tactile warning strips on platform edges
[p393_c0011] _Page 38_
[p393_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p393_c0013] _Master Page # 393 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 394

### [p394_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
- [p394_c0002] • Accessible restrooms • ADA parking (for stations with parking facilities) • Street level curb cuts • Directional signage for ADA accessible path through station
[p394_c0003] The LIRR reaches out to local municipalities for their support in providing adequate accessible parking and other municipal services.
### [p394_c0004] G. Station Appearance
[p394_c0005] The following matrix defines maintenance service provided by the LIRR based on the level of customer use:
[p394_c0006]

```text
Level 1 Level 2 Level 3 Level 4/5 Regular Station Cleaning Twice Daily Daily Daily Alternate Days Heavy Duty Cleaning 1 ½ Months 3 Months 4 Months As needed Graffiti Removal 48 Hours 72 Hours 72 Hours 84 Hours Station Painting 4 Years 6 Years 8 Years 10 Years Station Safety Audit 6 times/yr 4 times/yr 3 times/yr 2 times/yr
```
[p394_c0007] Notes:
- [p394_c0008] 1. Regular station cleaning includes cleaning station buildings and ticket offices, waiting rooms, customer shelters, and platforms to a litter-free level of cleanliness; emptying trash receptacles and sweeping, mopping, and stocking public restrooms, where provided. 2. Heavy Duty cleaning includes steam and chemical spray cleaning of masonry structures, shelters, staircases, platforms and waiting rooms, as weather conditions permit. 3. “Graffiti removal” refers to the response time from receipt of complaint to actual removal of graffiti. In addition, the railroad will apply an anti-graffiti sealing compound to all station masonry structures in conjunction with the completion of that station’s painting program. 4. Obscene graffiti is removed within 18 hours of notification 5. In addition to Station Painting which is done on a regular cycle, Branch Line Managers submit work orders to Engineering for any supplemental painting and repair that needs to be undertaken. 6. Branch Line Managers and Terminal Managers conduct Station Safety Audits, inspecting every aspect of the station environment. In addition, System Safety and the Structures Division of the Engineering Department also conduct station inspections. 7. Station painting maintenance service applies to stations where remediation for lead paint and asbestos has already been completed.
[p394_c0009] _Page 39_
[p394_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p394_c0011] _Master Page # 394 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 403

### [p403_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p403_c0002] A.6. Minority-Based Analysis – Station Amenities
### [p403_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p403_c0004] Chi-Square analysis was performed on station amenities with established standards and guidelines to determine if there is any difference in amenity distribution between the stations used in the minority and non-minority census tracts.
[p403_c0005] Based on the chi-square results, we can conclude that there appears to be no disparity in the amenities provided at all the stations that were listed in the minority and non-minority census tracts. The results of the chi-square analysis are shown in Table 1 below.
[p403_c0006] _Page 48_
[p403_c0007] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p403_c0008] _Master Page # 403 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 405

### [p405_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p405_c0002] B.1. Income-Based Analysis – Service Availability
### [p405_c0003] Data
[p405_c0004] Service Availability is a general measure of the distribution of routes within the MTA Long Island Rail Road (LIRR) service area.
[p405_c0005] In order to compare the distance traveled to gain access to LIRR service between the at or below poverty and above poverty areas, the New York City and Long Island census tracts were separated into at or below poverty and above poverty tracts.
[p405_c0006] LIRR service area includes all census tracts that are within (and touching) 2.5 miles of all LIRR railroad stations in Manhattan, Queens, Brooklyn, Nassau and Suffolk counties.
[p405_c0007] For the LIRR New York City service area (of the 5 counties making up New York City, the LIRR serves 3: Queens, Kings (Brooklyn), and New York (Manhattan)), a census tract is considered at or below poverty level if the at or below poverty population of the tract is greater than or equal to the service area at or below poverty population of 14.27%. For the Long Island counties of Nassau and Suffolk (the LIRR serves both), a census tract is considered at or below poverty level if the at or below poverty population of the tract is greater than or equal to the service area at or below poverty population of 6.02%. These thresholds, and subsequent income-based analysis, are based on 2018-2022 ACS Data.
[p405_c0008] Ten percent of all the LIRR service area census tracts were randomly selected for analysis ( 5% from the at or below poverty tracts and 5% from the above poverty tracts). The distance from the centroid of each census tract to the closest LIRR station was measured. Additionally, our sampling methodology was done by region, (20% NYC and 80% LIRR service areas) reflecting LIRR ridership patterns.
[p405_c0009] Table 2 lists each randomly selected tract number (grouped by poverty status), the distance to the closest LIRR station from the centroid of the tract, and the closest LIRR station
[p405_c0010] _Page 50_

## Page 416

### [p416_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p416_c0002] B.1. Income-Based Analysis – Service Availability (Continued)
### [p416_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p416_c0004] To determine if the LIRR service provided is equitable with respect to at or below poverty and above poverty census tracts, a comparison of service availability was performed. A t -test statistical analysis was used to see if there was any difference in the distances to the closest railroad stations, between the randomly selected Above Poverty and the At or Below poverty populations.
[p416_c0005] Based on the results below, we can conclude that there appears to be no inequity between the Above and the At or Below poverty populations as it relates to service availability in the LIRR service areas.
[p416_c0006]

```text
ABOVE POVERTY AT OR BELOW POVERTY Mean Distance (miles) 1.40 1.38 Standard Deviation (miles) 0.79 0.88
```
[p416_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (for a 95% confidence), the resulting t -statistic = -0.20. The t -critical values are +/- 1.972. Since -0.20 > -1.972 and < +1.972, we can therefore conclude that there appears to be no significant difference in the distance to the closest LIRR station for both Above poverty and the At or Below poverty populations within the LIRR service area.
[p416_c0008] _Page 61_
[p416_c0009] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p416_c0010] _Master Page # 416 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 417

### [p417_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p417_c0002] B.2. Income-Based Analysis – Vehicle Load Factor
### [p417_c0003] Data
[p417_c0004] The Long Island Rail Road load factor standards are designed to be responsive to passenger demands. The overall load factor standard is one (1) train car for every 115* boarding passengers on M3 electric cars, one (1) train car for every 106* boarding passengers on M7 and M9 electric cars. As of June 30, 2024, 9% of the available MU (multiple unit) fleet was M3 cars, 73% was M7 cars and 18% was M9 cars. Therefore, the average number of seats per car was calculated as 107.5.
[p417_c0005] In addition, the overall load factor standard is one (1) train car for every 140 boarding passengers on diesel bi-level cars per station east of Jamaica. A review of train size is initiated when train loading exceeds or falls below established load point factors. The LIRR goal is to provide adequate seating during the morning and evening peak period. The Long Island Rail Road monitors and reviews vehicle load data on an on-going basis and, wherever feasible, reallocates human and equipment resources to meet passenger demand.
[p417_c0006] Table 1 is the load factor analysis based on the 2024 Ridership Count 2 . This table illustrates that the LIRR has been responsive to passenger seating demands. The table shows the peak one (1) hour period load factor for all branches on the LIRR. Peak hour trains are defined as those trains arriving at the western terminals, or providing connecting service to trains arriving at western terminals, between 8:00 a.m. and 9:00 a.m.
[p417_c0007] Table 2 shows the off-peak period load factor for all branches on the LIRR. Off-Peak is defined as those trains arriving at the western terminals, or providing connecting service to trains arriving at western terminals, between 10:00 a.m. and 4:00 p.m.
[p417_c0008] Note: A branch is denoted as an At or Below Poverty branch if at least one third of its total route mileage falls in one or more At or Below Poverty census tracts. Any station that is located within a minority census tract is considered a minority station.
[p417_c0009] *Number of seats on M3, M7 and M9 cars is an average. M7 and M9 cars seating varies, one car in each pair is equipped with a handicapped accessible restroom and therefore has less seats than its mate. In addition, minor modifications have been undertaken to increase seating on some M7 cars.
[p417_c0010] _2 Data that was available as of 6/30/2024_
[p417_c0011] _Page 62_
[p417_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p417_c0013] _Master Page # 417 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

### [p418_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
[p418_c0002] _TABLE 1_

### [p418_c0004] 6/30/2025 BRANCH LOAD FACTORS – AM PEAK HOUR
[p418_c0005]

```text
BRANCH NO. OF TRAINS NO. OF CARS NO. OF SEATS NO. OF PSGRS LOAD FACTOR * Babylon 9 90 9,675 6,420 0.66 Far Rockaway 5 40 4300 1700 0.4 Hempstead 2 16 1,720 1,260 0.73 * Huntington/Hicksville 4 42 4,515 3,080 0.68 * Long Beach 4 38 4,085 2,070 0.51 Oyster Bay 2 10 1,400 1,090 0.78 Patchogue/Speonk/Montauk 4 20 2,800 1,740 0.62 * Port Jefferson 3 16 2,240 1,520 0.68 * Port Washington 6 64 6,880 5,180 0.75 * Ronkonkoma/Greenport 5 50 5,440 3,820 0.7 West Hempstead 2 16 1720 1260 0.45 SYSTEM TOTAL 46 400 44,560 28,560 0.64
```
[p418_c0006] (1) Bi-Level = 140 seats /car

## Page 419

### [p419_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p419_c0002] B.2. Income-Based Analysis – Vehicle Load Factor
### [p419_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p419_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the services provided for the at or below poverty and the above poverty branches. The results for the am peak and off peak time periods are listed below.
### [p419_c0005] AM PEAK
[p419_c0006]

```text
AT/BELOW POVERTY ABOVE POVERTY Guideline Average Load Factor 0.95 0.95 Actual Average Load Factor 0.61 0.65 Average Difference 0.34 0.30 Variance 0.029 0.007
```
[p419_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -0.54 . The t-critical values are +/-2.45. Since -0.54 > -2.45 and < +2.45, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the AM peak train service provided between the at or below poverty and above poverty branches
[p419_c0008] _Page 64_
[p419_c0009] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p419_c0010] _Master Page # 419 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 420

### [p420_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p420_c0002] B.3. Income-Based Analysis – Vehicle Headway
### [p420_c0003] Data
[p420_c0004] In determining train headways, many factors are considered. The first is ridership demand. Service is scheduled to ensure that, where possible, all customers can expect a seated ride to their destination. Minimum levels of service to ensure customer convenience and comfort are based on Long Island Rail Road Service Guidelines. Annual ridership checks are performed on all scheduled trains to determine if LIRR service meets current ridership demand and Service Guideline standards.
[p420_c0005] In addition to ridership demand, vehicle headways are significantly influenced by crew and equipment availability. After determining the proper number of trains per hour based on ridership demand, the equipment to be operated and the crews to operate the equipment must be manipulated throughout the LIRR system to assure that all train intervals can be covered with maximum efficiency.
[p420_c0006] The number of available train slots within our system influences the number of trains that can be scheduled. Vehicle headways and capacity can be influenced by construction projects that affect infrastructure availability. The available infrastructure in a given area determines the maximum number of trains that can operate over a given portion of railroad. In some instances available time slots can also be impacted by service in the opposing direction. Vehicles headways are scheduled to meet Service Guidelines while assuring that the number and frequency of trains does not exceed the track and/or signal capacity in that given area.
[p420_c0007] The passenger train schedules provide the most current information as it pertains to the LIRR's vehicle headways. The schedules indicate the scheduled frequency of service provided by the LIRR. The LIRR considers the morning peak to be from 6:00 am until 10:00 am and the evening peak to be from 4:00 pm to 8:00 pm during the weekday.
[p420_c0008] The following table lists the actual average branch headways as of June 30, 2025.
[p420_c0009] _Page 65_
[p420_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p420_c0011] _Master Page # 420 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 421

### [p421_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p421_c0002] AVERAGE VEHICLE HEADWAY (minutes)
[p421_c0003]

```text
BRANCH AM PEAK PM PEAK OFF PEAK * BABYLON 9 11 19 FAR ROCKAWAY 17 28 53 HEMPSTEAD 25 25 58 * HUNTINGTON 15 18 30 * LONG BEACH 18 24 55 OYSTER BAY 39 50 75 * PATCHOGUE/SPEONK/MONTAUK 26 22 57 PORT JEFFERSON 27 33 71 * PORT WASHINGTON 16 13 29 * RONKONKOMA ^^ 12 14 29 WEST HEMPSTEAD 52 40 59
```
[p421_c0004] NOTE: Train stopping patterns vary to equalize passenger loadings and minimize standees.
[p421_c0005] * Designated as an At or Below Poverty branch
[p421_c0006] ^^ East of Ronkonkoma service was not included in the vehicle headway average minutes analysis.
[p421_c0007] _Page 66_
[p421_c0008] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p421_c0009] _Master Page # 421 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 422

### [p422_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p422_c0002] B.3. Income-Based Analysis – Vehicle Headway
### [p422_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p422_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the level of service provided for the at or below poverty routes and the above poverty routes. We can conclude from the following analysis that there appears to be no inequity as it relates to average vehicle headway in LIRR’s service area. The results for the am peak and off peak time periods are listed below.
### [p422_c0005] AM PEAK
[p422_c0006]

```text
AT/BELOW POVERTY ABOVE POVERTY Actual Average Headway (minutes) 16 32 Standard Deviation 12.23 5.32
```
[p422_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -2.44 . The t-critical values are +/-2.57. Since -2.44 < -2.57, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the AM peak headway between the at or below poverty and above poverty branches.
### [p422_c0008] OFF PEAK
[p422_c0009]

```text
AT/BELOW POVERTY ABOVE POVERTY Actual Average Headway (minutes) 36.5 63.2 Standard Deviation 8.35 14.28
```
[p422_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -3.50 . The t-critical values are +/-2.31. Since -3.50 > 2.31, we can therefore conclude that there is a significant difference in the average headway during the off peak on the at or below poverty and above poverty branches, in favor of the at or below poverty branches.
[p422_c0011] _Page 67_
[p422_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p422_c0013] _Master Page # 422 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 423

### [p423_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p423_c0002] B.4. Income-Based Analysis – On-Time Performance
### [p423_c0003] Data
[p423_c0004] On-time performance is a measure of runs completed as scheduled. At the Long Island Rail Road, a train is recorded as on time if it arrives at its destination within five minutes and 59 seconds of its scheduled arrival. 100% of trains operated should complete their assigned trips. Unless noted on the timetable, trains will not depart early from passenger stations where they are scheduled to receive passengers.
[p423_c0005] The table below lists the Peak and Off Peak Total Branch On-Time Performance for the year preceding June 30, 2025. This will be the basis of our equity analysis.
### [p423_c0006] TOTAL BRANCH ON-TIME PERFORMANCE
[p423_c0007] (7/1/24 06/30/25)
[p423_c0008]

```text
BRANCH AM PEAK OFF PEAK * BABYLON 93.5 94.8 * FAR ROCKAWAY 95.4 98 HEMPSTEAD 92.9 96.3 * HUNT. HICKS. 93.4 95.8 LONG BEACH 94.9 97.6 MONTAUK 95 92.2 * OYSTER BAY 94.4 94.6 * PORT JEFFERSON 90.2 92.1 PORT WASHINGTON 96.9 97.9 RONK. (GREENPT.) 93.1 97.9 * WEST HEMPSTEAD 94.7 96.5
```
[p423_c0009] * Designated as an At or Below Poverty branch
[p423_c0010] _Page 68_
[p423_c0011] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p423_c0012] _Master Page # 423 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 424

### [p424_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p424_c0002] B.4. Income-Based Analysis – On-Time Performance
### [p424_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p424_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the on-time performance of vehicles on the at or below poverty routes and the above poverty routes. We can conclude from the following analysis that there appears to be no inequity as it relates to on-time performance in LIRR’s service area. The result of the analysis is listed below.
### [p424_c0005] Total On-Time Performance – AM PEAK
[p424_c0006]

```text
AT/BELOW POVERTY ABOVE POVERTY Average On-Time Performance 94.47 93.52 Standard Deviation 0.01 0.02
```
[p424_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 0.86 . The t-critical values are +/-2.36. Since 0.86 > -2.36 and < +2.36, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the am peak average on-time performance of vehicles on the at or below poverty and above poverty branches.
### [p424_c0008] Total On-Time Performance – OFF PEAK
[p424_c0009]

```text
AT/BELOW POVERTY ABOVE POVERTY Average On-Time Performance 95.35 96.6 Standard Deviation 0.01 0.03
```
[p424_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -1.30 . The t-critical values are +/-2.31. Since -1.30 > -2.31 and < +2.31, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the off peak average on-time performance of vehicles on the at or below poverty and above poverty.
[p424_c0011] _Page 69_
[p424_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p424_c0013] _Master Page # 424 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 425

### [p425_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p425_c0002] B.5. Income-Based Analysis – Vehicle Assignment
### [p425_c0003] Data
[p425_c0004] Electric Multiple Unit (MU) passenger cars are operated on the branches with electrification. As of June 30, 2025, the LIRR owned 1130 electric MU cars. 956 of these cars were required to operate morning rush hour service.
[p425_c0005] The portion of the line that is not electrified operates with diesel hauled locomotive coaches. As of June 30, 2025, the LIRR owned 134 diesel-hauled cars. A weekday average of 116 cars were required to operate the scheduled diesel service.
[p425_c0006] Both electric and diesel cars are assigned as required without regard to car age. Electric and diesel cars have the same amenities - air conditioning, heating and restrooms for passenger comfort and convenience. The average age of the M-3 electric cars is 38 years, the M-7 electric cars is 20 years, and the M-9 electric cars is 4.2 years. The average age of the diesel cars is 27 years.
[p425_c0007] Cars are not assigned to specific routes or branches within electric or diesel territory but are cycled from branch to branch and rotated based on a car maintenance schedule. To achieve optimum car utilization efficiency of both electric and diesel equipment, cars are cycled from branch to branch on a daily basis. For example, an early morning peak train from Babylon to Penn Station is cycled back to Port Washington for a second trip from Port Washington to Penn Station.
[p425_c0008] Table 1 is the Vehicle Assignment Record as of June 30, 2025.
[p425_c0009] _Page 70_
[p425_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p425_c0011] _Master Page # 425 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 427

### [p427_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p427_c0002] B.5. Income-Based Analysis – Vehicle Assignment
### [p427_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p427_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the average age of the equipment used on the at or below poverty and the above poverty branches. The results for the am peak and off peak time periods are listed below. The results for both time periods are the same because the same equipment is used throughout the day.
### [p427_c0005] AM Peak
[p427_c0006]

```text
AT/BELOW POVERTY ABOVE POVERTY Average Age (years) 20.03 21.98 Standard Deviation 3.34 4.10
```
[p427_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 0.79 . The t-critical values are +/-2.36. Since 0.79 > -2.36 and < +2.36, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the average age of the equipment used during the AM peak on the at or below poverty and the above poverty branches.
### [p427_c0008] Off-Peak
[p427_c0009]

```text
AT/BELOW POVERTY ABOVE POVERTY Average Age (years) 20.03 21.98 Standard Deviation 3.34 4.10
```
[p427_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 0.79 . The t-critical values are +/-2.36. Since 0.79 > -2.36 and < +2.36, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the average age of the equipment used during the off-peak on the at or below poverty and the above poverty branches.
[p427_c0011] _Page 72_
[p427_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p427_c0013] _Master Page # 427 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 428

### [p428_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p428_c0002] B.6. Income-Based Analysis – Station Amenities
### [p428_c0003] Data
[p428_c0004] The following guidelines are primarily based on passenger utilization of stations and represent basic levels of service the LIRR will add to if necessary, commensurate with individual station needs:
### [p428_c0005] A. Station Levels of Service
[p428_c0006] The Station Levels of Service are a measure of the number of customers that utilize a particular station each weekday. The Long Island Rail Road uses Station Levels of Service to allocate the appropriate level of service for such areas as station maintenance, station amenities, schedule frequency, and other areas. Station Levels of Service provide an equitable way of allocating operating and capital resources within the LIRR. Station levels of service are based upon the data obtained from the most recent station-by-station boarding counts.
[p428_c0007] Level 1: More than 6,000 customers a day
[p428_c0008] Level 2: 2,000 – 6,000 customers a day
[p428_c0009] Level 3: 1,000 –1,999 customers a day
[p428_c0010] Level 4: 100 –999 customers a day
[p428_c0011] Level 5: Fewer than 100 customers a day
### [p428_c0012] B. Transit Access and Station Location
[p428_c0013] Transit access is a measure of the distance a person must travel to gain access to MTA Long Island Rail Road commuting services.
[p428_c0014] The decision to add, relocate, or close a passenger station on a permanent basis is a rare occurrence and is undertaken only after detailed evaluation of environmental, economic and operational impacts, and analysis of current station based boarding counts. These guidelines will serve to address shifting ridership patterns or opportunities associated with major service changes (e.g. electrification of a branch or branch segment).
[p428_c0015] When conditions exist that warrant development of new station sites, a review will be undertaken using the following criteria:
- [p428_c0016] • Ridership Potential • Operational Feasibility • Availability of Capital and Operating Funds • Community Impact • Environmental Justice impacts • Parking Availability • Proximity to existing LIRR stations • Other nearby transit options
[p428_c0017] When customer volume through a station falls below 400 passengers a day, a review will be initiated to determine the feasibility of diverting those passengers to neighboring stations and closing the station to thereby reduce trip time on the affected branch.
[p428_c0018] _Page 73_
[p428_c0019] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p428_c0020] _Master Page # 428 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 429

### [p429_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p429_c0002] C. Distribution of Transit Amenities
[p429_c0003] Transit amenities are items of comfort and convenience made available to the riding public at LIRR stations, including escalators, elevators, pedestrian overpasses / tunnels, waiting rooms, canopies/shelters, restrooms, staffed-ticket offices, ticket vending machines, platform shelters, public telephones, and vending concessions. The LIRR has developed station standards (shown below), which are utilized to determine the level of service that will be provided at each station. The level of amenities is based on station use level, i.e. the greater the number of passengers using a station on a daily basis, the greater the level of amenities provided at that station. Stations are categorized by level, ranging from Level 1 to Level 5, with Level 1 station serving more than 6,000 customers per day and a Level 4 station serving less than 100 customers per day. A Level 1 station receives a full range of transit amenities.
### [p429_c0004] D. Station Parking
[p429_c0005] Station parking projects are designed to improve vehicular access, to provide safer, more convenient intermodal transfers, to maximize parking capacity, and generally, to enhance the appearance of stations and surrounding areas.
[p429_c0006] Note: Local municipalities provide the majority of parking, some maintenance functions and various other services at LIRR stations. To support the municipal parking process, the LIRR provides station profiles to the responsible municipality when requested. Station profiles include characteristics of LIRR services offered at the station such as parking presently provided, parking lot lighting standards and present and projected station ridership data.
### [p429_c0007] E. Station Facilities
[p429_c0008] The following guidelines are primarily based on passenger utilization of stations and represent basic levels of service. The LIRR will add to if necessary, commensurate with individual station needs:
[p429_c0009] Level 1: More than 6,000 customers a day
[p429_c0010] Level 2: 2,000 – 6,000 customers a day
[p429_c0011] Level 3: 1,000 –1,999 customers a day
[p429_c0012] Level 4: 100 –999 customers a day
[p429_c0013] Level 5: Fewer than 100 customers a day
[p429_c0014] _Page 74_
[p429_c0015] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p429_c0016] _Master Page # 429 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 430

### [p430_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
[p430_c0002] The following matrix defines station facilities provided for each of the 5 Station Levels of Service:
[p430_c0003]

```text
Level 1 Level 2 Level 3 Level 4/5 Staffed Ticket Office X Public Restrooms X X Station Waiting Room X X X Ticket Vending Machine X X X X* Public Address Sytem X X X X Platform Shelters X X X X Customer Signage / Transit Information Center X X X X Platform Lighting X X X X
```
[p430_c0004] Notes:
- [p430_c0005] * Stations without TVMs include Mets-Willets Pt, Belmont Park, Pinelawn, Bellport, Yaphank, Riverhead, Mattituck, Southold, and Greenport. 5. There are three types of TVMs – Full Service TVMs which sell one-way, round-trip, weekly, ten-trip, and monthly tickets, as well as pre-valued MetroCards, and UniTicket. Daily TVMs sell one-way and round trip tickets and pre-valued MetroCards only. Cashless TVMs which sell all the ticket types that the Full Service TVMs offer, but accept only credit, debit and approved pre-tax commuter benefit cards. 6. Station waiting rooms are heated facilities; platform shelters are not. Some station platform waiting rooms are heated. 7. It is not the intent of the LIRR to reduce existing passenger amenities. Therefore, services will continue to be provided when station waiting rooms exist at level 4 stations and where public lavatories exist at level 3 and level 4 stations. 8. Special Event Stations are not included in the station level rankings as they do not have year-round train service. As a minimum, these stations will have passenger shelters, platforms, lighting in the vicinity of the shelters, and station signs. Other services will be provided as station use warrants.
### [p430_c0006] F. Station Accessibility
[p430_c0007] The LIRR is committed to complying with Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA) legislation and strives to make its rail system accessible to individuals with disabilities.
[p430_c0008] Of the 126 stations in the LIRR system, 116 of these stations have elevators and/or ramps to provide access to customers who use wheelchairs or other mobility aids. For example all stations on the Long Beach, West Hempstead (except St Albans), Montauk, Oyster Bay, and Ronkonkoma branches are wheelchair-accessible. Stations that are wheelchair-accessible are designated as such on all LIRR maps and timetables.
[p430_c0009] In addition to providing elevators and/or ramps, many stations have some of the following accessibility features, and all features are present at many stations:
- [p430_c0010] • ADA compliant handrails on ramps, stairs, and platforms • Braille signage • Audio and visual information systems • Accessible station ticket windows • Accessible Ticket Vending Machines (TVMs) • Tactile warning strips on platform edges
[p430_c0011] _Page 75_
[p430_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p430_c0013] _Master Page # 430 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 431

### [p431_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
- [p431_c0002] • Accessible restrooms • ADA parking (for stations with parking facilities) • Street level curb cuts • Directional signage for ADA accessible path through station
[p431_c0003] The LIRR reaches out to local municipalities for their support in providing adequate accessible parking and other municipal services.
### [p431_c0004] G. Station Appearance
[p431_c0005] The following matrix defines maintenance service provided by the LIRR based on the level of customer use:
[p431_c0006]

```text
Level 1 Level 2 Level 3 Level 4/5 Regular Station Cleaning Twice Daily Daily Daily Alternate Days Heavy Duty Cleaning 1 ½ Months 3 Months 4 Months As needed Graffiti Removal 48 Hours 72 Hours 72 Hours 84 Hours Station Painting 4 Years 6 Years 8 Years 10 Years Station Safety Audit 6 times/yr 4 times/yr 3 times/yr 2 times/yr
```
[p431_c0007] Notes:
- [p431_c0008] 1. Regular station cleaning includes cleaning station buildings and ticket offices, waiting rooms, customer shelters, and platforms to a litter-free level of cleanliness; emptying trash receptacles and sweeping, mopping, and stocking public restrooms, where provided. 2. Heavy Duty cleaning includes steam and chemical spray cleaning of masonry structures, shelters, staircases, platforms and waiting rooms, as weather conditions permit. 3. “Graffiti removal” refers to the response time from receipt of complaint to actual removal of graffiti. In addition, the railroad will apply an anti-graffiti sealing compound to all station masonry structures in conjunction with the completion of that station’s painting program. 4. Obscene graffiti is removed within 18 hours of notification 5. In addition to Station Painting which is done on a regular cycle, Branch Line Managers submit work orders to Engineering for any supplemental painting and repair that needs to be undertaken. 6. Branch Line Managers and Terminal Managers conduct Station Safety Audits, inspecting every aspect of the station environment. In addition, System Safety and the Structures Division of the Engineering Department also conduct station inspections. 7. Station painting maintenance service applies to stations where remediation for lead paint and asbestos has already been completed.
[p431_c0009] _Page 76_
[p431_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p431_c0011] _Master Page # 431 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 434

### [p434_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR

## Page 440

### [p440_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p440_c0002] B.6. Income-Based Analysis – Station Amenities
### [p440_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p440_c0004] Chi-Square analysis was performed on station amenities with established standards and guidelines to determine if there is any difference in amenity distribution between the stations used in the minority and non-minority census tracts.
[p440_c0005] Based on the chi-square results, we can conclude that there appears to be no disparity in the amenities provided at all the stations regardless of poverty status. The results of the chi-square analysis are shown in Table 1 below.
[p440_c0006] _Page 85_
[p440_c0007] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
